quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Glycolysis"?

options.text: A 10-step metabolic pathway in the cytoplasm that converts glucose (6C) into 2 pyruvate (3C), producing 2 net ATP and 2 NADH
v = Vmax[S]/(Km + [S]); describes the relationship between substrate concentration and reaction rate for many enzymes
Both alleles are fully expressed in the heterozygote (e.g., Iᴬ Iᴮ blood type produces both A and B antigens)
A cluster of prokaryotic genes under control of a single promoter and operator, transcribed as one polycistronic mRNA (e.g., lac operon, trp operon)

quiz_questions.explanation: "Glycolysis" is defined as: A 10-step metabolic pathway in the cytoplasm that converts glucose (6C) into 2 pyruvate (3C), producing 2 net ATP and 2 NADH. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Substrate-level phosphorylation"?

options.text: Direct transfer of a phosphate group from a substrate molecule to ADP to form ATP; occurs in glycolysis and the Krebs cycle
Germline mutations (in eggs/sperm) are passed to offspring; somatic mutations affect only the individual and are not inherited
A grid used to predict genotype and phenotype ratios in offspring from a particular cross
A gene encoding a protein that inhibits cell division (e.g., p53 triggers apoptosis in damaged cells, Rb blocks G1/S transition)

quiz_questions.explanation: "Substrate-level phosphorylation" is defined as: Direct transfer of a phosphate group from a substrate molecule to ADP to form ATP; occurs in glycolysis and the Krebs cycle. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Phosphofructokinase (PFK)"?

options.text: Both alleles are fully expressed in the heterozygote (e.g., Iᴬ Iᴮ blood type produces both A and B antigens)
Cyclins accumulate and activate CDKs at specific cell cycle stages, driving progression through phosphorylation of target proteins
The enzyme that synthesizes ATP as H⁺ flows down its concentration gradient from the intermembrane space back to the matrix
The key allosteric regulatory enzyme of glycolysis; inhibited by ATP and citrate (when energy is abundant), activated by AMP and ADP

quiz_questions.explanation: "Phosphofructokinase (PFK)" is defined as: The key allosteric regulatory enzyme of glycolysis; inhibited by ATP and citrate (when energy is abundant), activated by AMP and ADP. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "NAD⁺/NADH"?

options.text: Membrane-bound organelle containing chromatin (DNA + histones); site of DNA replication, transcription, and ribosome assembly (nucleolus)
Speciation occurring within the same geographic area, often through polyploidy (plants) or habitat/behavioral specialization
The covalent bond between the 3' carbon of one nucleotide's sugar and the 5' phosphate of the next, forming the sugar-phosphate backbone
An electron carrier; NAD⁺ is reduced to NADH during glycolysis (and other steps), carrying electrons to the electron transport chain

quiz_questions.explanation: "NAD⁺/NADH" is defined as: An electron carrier; NAD⁺ is reduced to NADH during glycolysis (and other steps), carrying electrons to the electron transport chain. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Fermentation"?

options.text: Anaerobic regeneration of NAD⁺ from NADH; alcohol fermentation produces ethanol + CO₂; lactic acid fermentation produces lactate
Exchange of genetic material between homologous chromosomes during prophase I of meiosis; increases genetic variation
Repair of a single damaged base: DNA glycosylase removes the damaged base, AP endonuclease cuts the backbone, and polymerase fills the gap
Two rounds of nuclear division producing four haploid gametes (2n → n); includes crossing over and independent assortment for genetic diversity

quiz_questions.explanation: "Fermentation" is defined as: Anaerobic regeneration of NAD⁺ from NADH; alcohol fermentation produces ethanol + CO₂; lactic acid fermentation produces lactate. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "A 10-step metabolic pathway in the cytoplasm that converts glucose (6C) into 2 pyruvate (3C), producing 2 net ATP and 2 NADH"?

options.text: CRISPR-Cas9
Parental vs. recombinant
Outgroup
Glycolysis

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Glycolysis".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Direct transfer of a phosphate group from a substrate molecule to ADP to form ATP; occurs in glycolysis and the Krebs cycle"?

options.text: Recombination frequency
Tertiary structure
Metastasis
Substrate-level phosphorylation

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Substrate-level phosphorylation".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "The key allosteric regulatory enzyme of glycolysis; inhibited by ATP and citrate (when energy is abundant), activated by AMP and ADP"?

options.text: Leading strand
Adaptive radiation
Missense mutation
Phosphofructokinase (PFK)

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Phosphofructokinase (PFK)".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "An electron carrier; NAD⁺ is reduced to NADH during glycolysis (and other steps), carrying electrons to the electron transport chain"?

options.text: Nonsense mutation
CRISPR-Cas9
Outgroup
NAD⁺/NADH

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "NAD⁺/NADH".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Anaerobic regeneration of NAD⁺ from NADH; alcohol fermentation produces ethanol + CO₂; lactic acid fermentation produces lactate"?

options.text: microRNA (miRNA)
Leading strand
Denaturation
Fermentation

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Fermentation".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Cellular Respiration: Glycolysis is accurate?

options.text: In eukaryotes, regulation occurs at multiple levels: chromatin remodeling (acetylation, methylation), transcription factors, mRNA processing, mRNA stability, translation regulation, and post-translational modification.
Cellular Respiration: Glycolysis occurs in the cytoplasm and breaks down one glucose molecule into two pyruvate molecules, producing a net gain of 2 ATP (substrate-level phosphorylation) and 2 NADH.
While most mutations are neutral or harmful, some confer advantages (e.g., sickle cell heterozygote advantage against malaria).
Mutation and Cancer explains how accumulated mutations in proto-oncogenes (gain-of-function to oncogenes) and tumor suppressor genes (loss-of-function) drive the multistep development of cancer.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Cellular Respiration: Glycolysis as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Cellular Respiration: Glycolysis?

options.text: Mutation and Cancer explains how accumulated mutations in proto-oncogenes (gain-of-function to oncogenes) and tumor suppressor genes (loss-of-function) drive the multistep development of cancer.
Glycolysis is anaerobic (does not require oxygen) and is the most evolutionarily ancient metabolic pathway, occurring in virtually all living organisms.

Cellular Respiration (overview) is the complete oxidation of glucose through glycolysis, pyruvate oxidation, the Krebs cycle, and oxidative phosphorylation, yielding approximately 30-32 ATP per glucose molecule.
Tumor suppressors (p53, Rb) and proto-oncogenes regulate these checkpoints.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Cellular Respiration: Glycolysis as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Cellular Respiration: Glycolysis?

options.text: When damage is too severe, p53 activates apoptosis to prevent propagation of mutations that could lead to cancer.
Population Ecology studies how populations grow, interact with their environment, and are regulated.
The 10-step pathway is regulated at key irreversible steps by allosteric enzymes (hexokinase, PFK, pyruvate kinase).
While most mutations are neutral or harmful, some confer advantages (e.g., sickle cell heterozygote advantage against malaria).

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Cellular Respiration: Glycolysis as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Cellular Respiration: Glycolysis?

options.text: Cellular Respiration: Glycolysis occurs in the cytoplasm and breaks down one glucose molecule into two pyruvate molecules, producing a net gain of 2 ATP (substrate-level phosphorylation) and 2 NADH.
DNA Replication and Transcription describes how genetic information stored in DNA is copied (replication) and read into mRNA (transcription).
Triglycerides (fats and oils) store energy (9 kcal/g) through ester bonds between glycerol and three fatty acids.
The central dogma of molecular biology—DNA → RNA → Protein—describes the flow of genetic information in cells.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Cellular Respiration: Glycolysis as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Cellular Respiration: Glycolysis?

options.text: Phospholipids form the basis of cell membrane bilayers due to their amphipathic nature (hydrophilic head, hydrophobic tails).
Glycolysis is anaerobic (does not require oxygen) and is the most evolutionarily ancient metabolic pathway, occurring in virtually all living organisms.
Population viability analysis (PVA) estimates extinction probability for small populations considering demographic and environmental stochasticity.
When damage is too severe, p53 activates apoptosis to prevent propagation of mutations that could lead to cancer.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Cellular Respiration: Glycolysis as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Cellular Respiration: Glycolysis?

options.text: Xeroderma pigmentosum caused by defective nucleotide excision repair, making patients extremely sensitive to UV radiation-induced DNA damage
Lactic acid fermentation in muscles during intense exercise when oxygen supply is insufficient for aerobic respiration, causing muscle fatigue and soreness
The Human Genome Project using genetic and physical mapping to determine the positions of all ~20,000 human genes across 23 chromosome pairs
Captive breeding and reintroduction programs recovering the California condor from 22 individuals (1982) to over 500, including 300+ in the wild

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Cellular Respiration: Glycolysis.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Cellular Respiration: Glycolysis?

options.text: Lynch syndrome (hereditary nonpolyposis colorectal cancer) caused by inherited mutations in mismatch repair genes, increasing cancer risk
Brewing and winemaking using yeast alcohol fermentation to convert glucose to ethanol and CO₂
HPV (human papillomavirus) causing cervical cancer by producing proteins that inactivate p53 and Rb tumor suppressors, disabling cell cycle checkpoints
Madagascar's unique biodiversity (90% of species found nowhere else) explained by 88 million years of isolation after separating from Africa and India

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Cellular Respiration: Glycolysis.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Cellular Respiration: Glycolysis applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: Artificial photosynthesis research mimicking PSII's water-splitting capability to generate hydrogen fuel using sunlight
Cancer cell metabolism (Warburg effect): many cancer cells preferentially use glycolysis even in the presence of oxygen, a target for anticancer drug development

Epinephrine (adrenaline) signaling cascade: one epinephrine molecule activates many G-proteins, each activating adenylyl cyclase producing millions of cAMP molecules, amplifying the fight-or-flight response
Forensic genetics using Mendelian inheritance patterns to establish paternity or identify family relationships from DNA evidence

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Cellular Respiration: Glycolysis.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Cellular Respiration: Glycolysis?

options.text: Cancer immunotherapy using checkpoint inhibitors (anti-PD-1, anti-CTLA-4) to re-enable the immune system's ability to attack cancer cells
The Human Genome Project using genetic and physical mapping to determine the positions of all ~20,000 human genes across 23 chromosome pairs
Lactic acid fermentation in muscles during intense exercise when oxygen supply is insufficient for aerobic respiration, causing muscle fatigue and soreness
Cancer epigenetics: abnormal DNA methylation patterns silencing tumor suppressor genes or activating oncogenes without DNA mutations

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Cellular Respiration: Glycolysis.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Cellular Respiration: Glycolysis in practice?

options.text: DNA fingerprinting using STR analysis for forensic identification, with Combined DNA Index System (CODIS) databases matching crime scene samples to individuals
Lac operon as a model for understanding antibiotic resistance gene regulation in bacteria, where environmental signals trigger expression of resistance genes
Firefly bioluminescence using ATP to power the oxidation of luciferin, converting chemical energy directly to light with remarkable efficiency
Brewing and winemaking using yeast alcohol fermentation to convert glucose to ethanol and CO₂

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Cellular Respiration: Glycolysis.
